title: SellIntent lifecycle gap breaks the per-symbol single-mandate invariant across a session boundary, permitting two simultaneously ACTIVE envelopes to stage overlapping full-size SELL orders against one position
module: app/store/memory.py, app/store/sqlite.py, app/store/core.py (envelope activation + close_session + sell_intent_is_active)
location: app/store/memory.py:1287-1360 (approve_envelope_activation), :2919-2925 (_close_session_unlocked open_sell_intents filter); app/store/sqlite.py:4261-4272 (mirrors it); app/store/core.py sell_intent_is_active / INV-031-032
mechanism: No store operation in the envelope lifecycle (create_envelope / approve_envelope_activation / stage_envelope_action / record_envelope_fill) ever transitions the driving SellIntent to ORDERED or touches it at all -- that transition only exists on the legacy create_order_for_sell_intent path. So an envelope-driven SellIntent sits at APPROVED for the entire life of its envelope. `_close_session_unlocked` (both stores) treats any SellIntent still in (PENDING, APPROVED) as an abandoned, never-dispatched intent and EXPIREs it at session close -- even though its envelope is still ACTIVE/FROZEN and legitimately working real orders. Once EXPIRED, `sell_intent_is_active`/INV-031's per-symbol single-flight dedup in `create_sell_intent` no longer sees it, so the very next `create_sell_intent` call for that symbol (a routine next-session manual flatten or protection-floor re-trigger) is NOT deduped against it. A human can then approve a brand-new ExecutionEnvelope for the new intent while the OLD envelope (still ACTIVE, still fully entitled to its original qty_ceiling) is untouched -- `approve_envelope_activation`'s only exclusivity check (`_other_active_envelope_unlocked`) is scoped to `sell_intent_id`, not `symbol`, so it does not see the clash. Both envelopes can then independently pass `stage_envelope_action`'s reduce-only check (each reads the CURRENT, still-100% live position under its own lock hold) and each mint a full-size CREATED SELL order -- two live orders whose combined quantity exceeds the actual position, exactly the double-book / TOCTOU setup the single-active-mandate design (INV-031/032, ADR-010 single-ACTIVE invariant) exists to prevent.
adversarial_probe: Reproduced directly against InMemoryStateStore: BUY-fill AAPL to a 100-share position; create SellIntent#1 (MANUAL_FLATTEN, symbol=AAPL) -> approve -> approve_envelope_activation gives Envelope#1 ACTIVE (qty_ceiling=100). Call close_session() (ordinary end-of-day). Envelope#1 stays ACTIVE with remaining_quantity=100, but SellIntent#1 flips to EXPIRED. In the new session, create_sell_intent(symbol='AAPL', ...) returns a BRAND NEW SellIntent#2 (not deduped to #1) -> approve -> approve_envelope_activation succeeds, giving Envelope#2 ACTIVE for the SAME symbol. store.list_envelopes(symbol='AAPL') now shows 2 ACTIVE envelopes. Calling stage_envelope_action(Envelope#1.id, SUBMIT 100 shares) succeeds (outcome='staged', order qty=100), and calling stage_envelope_action(Envelope#2.id, SUBMIT 100 shares) ALSO succeeds (outcome='staged', order qty=100) -- two live CREATED SELL orders for 100 shares each now exist against a single 100-share position. If both reach the venue and either partially fills before the other is cancelled, the second order's eventual fill is a broker-authoritative overfill/short (ADR-001 quarantine territory) that never should have been reachable given the reduce-only/single-mandate design.

votes.reasoning: Every link of the probe traces cleanly through the real code at tip in BOTH stores. (1) No envelope operation advances the owning SellIntent past APPROVED — ORDERED is written only by the legacy create_order_for_sell_intent path (memory.py:1487, sqlite.py:2438); approve_envelope_activation/stage_envelope_action/record_envelope_fill never touch _sell_intents. So an envelope-backed intent sits at APPROVED for the mandate's whole life. (2) _close_session_unlocked (memory.py:2920-2925 select, 2972-2976 apply; sqlite mirror) EXPIRES every PENDING/APPROVED SellIntent for the session unconditionally, with no exclusion for one backed by an ACTIVE envelope — while envelopes are left untouched by close and CREATED SELL orders are deliberately kept alive past the bell (2908-2918). So Envelope#1 stays ACTIVE with remaining=100 while SellIntent#1 flips to EXPIRED. (3) sell_intent_is_active (core.py:792-798) returns False for EXPIRED, so create_sell_intent's per-symbol single-flight dedup no longer sees SI#1; a next-session create_sell_intent(AAPL) mints SI#2. (4) The single-ACTIVE exclusivity check is scoped to sell_intent_id, NOT symbol, in both stores: memory _other_active_envelope_unlocked (916-929) filters env.sell_intent_id==intent_id; the SQLite twin is a partial unique index ON execution_envelopes(sell_intent_id) WHERE status='active' (sqlite.py:503-504). Envelope#2 (SI#2) therefore does not clash with Envelope#1 (SI#1) and activates — two ACTIVE envelopes for one symbol, and dual-store parity (H10) holds so it is not caught there. (5) The reduce_only hard rail (core.py:2995) compares action.quantity to max(0, current_position) where current_position is the fill-derived projection (_position_unlocked), which CREATED/working SELL orders never move; _envelope_action_context_unlocked (memory.py:1166-1197) scopes working-order/structural detection to e.envelope_id==envelope.id. So each envelope independently reads position=100, sees no working order of its own, and stages a full-size 100-share SELL; two live CREATED SELLs (200 shares) now exist against a 100-share position. (6) monitoring.py drives envelopes by their own TTL/rails (665-692) with no tie to the owning intent's liveness, so the orphaned ACTIVE envelope is never cleaned up. The two eventual fills each stay within their own envelope's qty_ceiling=100, so the per-envelope ceiling/breach guard (H5) does not catch the combined oversell either. This defeats the reduce-only hard rail (H4) and the single-mandate design, producing a broker-authoritative oversell/short — precisely the quarantine territory the design exists to prevent. The enablers are structural (mis-scoped exclusivity + unconditional intent expiry + fill-only reduce_only), not a timing race, and the operator approving the second flatten envelope gets no clash signal because the invariant is scoped wrong. Severity P0 is accurate: silent double-book / oversell via single-mandate + reduce-only bypass. It falls in the reviewed delta's blast radius because WO-0026's reduce-only rail is exactly the guard H4 claims makes oversell impossible, and this refutes that safety-core hypothesis.
votes.counter_probe: Sought and could not find any symbol-scoped exclusivity or cross-envelope working-order accounting: memory.py _other_active_envelope_unlocked filters on sell_intent_id (916-929) and the sqlite partial unique index is ON(sell_intent_id) WHERE status='active' (sqlite.py:503-504) — neither is on symbol; reduce_only (core.py:2995) subtracts nothing for other envelopes' staged/working SELLs; _envelope_action_context_unlocked scopes working-order detection to the single envelope_id (memory.py:1173-1197); _close_session_unlocked has no clause sparing an APPROVED intent that owns an ACTIVE envelope (memory.py:2920-2925); and no reconciliation/monitoring path freezes an ACTIVE envelope when its owning SellIntent expires (monitoring.py:665-692 acts on envelope TTL/rails only). Any one of these guards would have refuted the probe; none exist.

Every link of the probe holds in the real code at tip:

The only writer of SellIntentStatus.ORDERED is the legacy create_order_for_sell_intent path (memory.py:1487, sqlite.py:2438). approve_envelope_activation / stage_envelope_action / record_envelope_fill never touch the driving intent. So an envelope's SellIntent sits at APPROVED for the envelope's whole life. CONFIRMED.

2. close_session expires it while the envelope lives on. _close_session_unlocked (memory.py:2920-2925) selects every SellIntent in (PENDING, APPROVED) and EXPIREs it (line 2972-2976). It does NOT touch envelopes at all — no envelope status is read or written in the close. So Envelope#1 stays ACTIVE with remaining_quantity=qty_ceiling=100 while SellIntent#1 flips to EXPIRED. CONFIRMED.

3. Dedup is defeated next session. create_sell_intent's single-flight guard calls _active_sell_intent_unlocked (memory.py:710), which uses sell_intent_is_active (core.py:792) — an EXPIRED intent returns False. So a new create_sell_intent for the same symbol returns a fresh SellIntent#2 rather than deduping to #1. CONFIRMED.

4. Second ACTIVE envelope for same symbol is allowed. approve_envelope_activation's only exclusivity check is _other_active_envelope_unlocked (memory.py:916, called at 1328), scoped strictly to sell_intent_id, not symbol. Two distinct intents => no clash => Envelope#2 goes ACTIVE for the same symbol. CONFIRMED.

5. Both envelopes are driven and both stage/submit full size. The monitoring pass iterates every ACTIVE envelope with NO per-symbol dedup (monitoring.py:571); its snap_memo is even keyed by symbol with the comment "shared across the symbol's envelopes" (line 614), so it anticipates multiple envelopes per symbol. Each _run_one_envelope builds history filtered to its OWN envelope_id (line 640-645) and _envelope_action_context_unlocked derives the working-order predicate per-envelope (memory.py:1173-1197, e.envelope_id == envelope.id) — so Env#2's SUBMIT does not see Env#1's live order and the structural max-outstanding=1 gate never fires across envelopes. stage_envelope_action's reduce-only rail reads the fill-derived position (memory.py:1242; core.py:2995 action.quantity > max(0, current_position)). Because venue fills are async and neither order has filled yet, both reads see position=100, so both stage full-size CREATED SELLs and execute_envelope_action submits both to the venue in the same pass. Two 100-share SELLs against a 100-share position => oversell/short — exactly the double-book the single-active-mandate design exists to prevent, landing in ADR-001 quarantine territory that was supposed to be structurally unreachable.

The redrive reduce-only re-check (reconciliation.py:698-703) is only a partial mitigation: it catches Env#2's order solely if Env#1's fill lands before Env#2 is driven. In a single tick both are staged+submitted before any fill, so the guard's fill-derived read is 100 for both and it does not fire.

Severity: P0 stands. This is a silent path to a broker-authoritative oversell / negative position that bypasses the reduce-only + single-mandate safety design (safety-core H4/H5). No gate catches it because ruff/mypy/pytest do not exercise the close_session -> new-session -> dual-active-envelope interleave.
votes.counter_probe: Decisive confirming facts: (a) memory.py:1487 shows ORDERED is written only on the legacy sell-intent order path, never the envelope path, so the driving intent is stuck at APPROVED; (b) _close_session_unlocked (memory.py:2920-2976) expires APPROVED intents and never touches envelopes; (c) _other_active_envelope_unlocked (memory.py:916-929) scopes the single-ACTIVE check to sell_intent_id, not symbol; (d) monitoring _run_one_envelope (monitoring.py:571, 614 "shared across the symbol's envelopes") drives every ACTIVE envelope with no per-symbol single-mandate check, and stage_envelope_action's reduce_only (core.py:2995) reads the fill-derived position which cannot observe a sibling in-flight SUBMIT — so two same-symbol envelopes each submit full-size before either fills. I searched for any symbol-scoped envelope guard in create_envelope/approve_envelope_activation/_run_one_envelope and found none; the only exclusivity is the sell_intent_id-scoped one, which the two-intent path sidesteps.

title: Envelope fill bridge fabricates a spurious overfill/quarantine audit fact on every clean exit (record-first breaks the pre-fill-position precondition of the overfill detector)
location: app/monitoring.py:1839 (record_envelope_fill before append_fill) and :2178; overfill check app/store/core.py:351 (plan_append_fill, would_go_negative(current_quantity,...)); current_quantity sourced from app/store/memory.py:2393 _position_unlocked -> app/events/projectors.py:117 project_symbol_position
mechanism: The WO-0020/WO-0025 record-first bridge calls store.record_envelope_fill(dedupe_key=fill:{order_id}:{sfid}) BEFORE store.append_fill for the same execution. record_envelope_fill appends the position-moving FILL ExecutionEvent to the log first. append_fill then computes current_quantity via project_symbol_position, which folds that just-written FILL event, so current_quantity is the POST-fill position. plan_append_fill's overfill detector assumes current_quantity is the PRE-fill position and computes is_overfill = would_go_negative(current_quantity, SELL, quantity) = quantity > current_quantity. Because the fill was already subtracted, any leg whose quantity exceeds the remaining-after position (every full protective exit; any leg > half the pre-fill remaining) is misclassified as a broker overfill: a fill_overfill_quarantined audit Event with payload quarantined=True is written for a legitimate flat exit. Position and the real quarantine latch stay correct only incidentally (quarantined_symbols independently re-derives from a NEGATIVE fold, which never occurs for a to-flat exit, and append_fill's shadow FILL event dedups to the record_envelope_fill event so position folds once). Both stores mislabel identically (H10 parity preserved), so ruff+mypy+pytest are all green.
adversarial_probe: scratchpad/probe2.py: BUY 100 AAA filled (position=100); approve+activate an envelope (remaining 100) for a MANUAL_FLATTEN intent; mint a SELL order for 100; then run the exact bridge sequence record_envelope_fill(env, qty=100, key=fill:{sell}:x1) THEN append_fill(sell, SELL, 100, sfid=x1). Observed: position after record_envelope_fill=0, position after append_fill=0 (correct), envelope=COMPLETED remaining=0 (correct), list_quarantined_symbols()=set() (real latch NOT set) — but a durable EVENT fill_overfill_quarantined {quantity:100, current_quantity:0, quarantined:True} is emitted for a clean 100->0 exit. Repeats for any leg quantity > (pre-fill remaining)/2.
invariant_violated: H5 / INV-085 (fidelity: overfill facts recorded faithfully, never fabricated/hidden)

votes.reasoning: Traced the full call path: monitoring.py's _apply_update (streamed fills, ~line 1839) and _apply_inferred_fills (inferred fills, ~line 2178) both call store.record_envelope_fill(...) BEFORE store.append_fill(...) for the same execution, using the same dedupe_key format fill:{order_id}:{source_fill_id}. record_envelope_fill (memory.py:1119, sqlite.py:2024) builds its fill_event via core.py's plan_envelope_fill (core.py:2525), which constructs an ExecutionEvent with event_type=ExecutionEventType.FILL, symbol=envelope.symbol, side=OrderSide.SELL, and appends it to the execution-event log FIRST (memory.py:1158 self._append_execution_event_unlocked(plan.fill_event)). append_fill (memory.py:2365, sqlite.py:3562) subsequently computes current_quantity by calling self._position_unlocked(key)/_position_locked(key) FRESH (memory.py:2393, sqlite.py:3599), which calls project_symbol_position (app/events/projectors.py:117) — this function folds ALL FILL-typed ExecutionEvents for the symbol in the log, including the one record_envelope_fill just wrote. So current_quantity passed into plan_append_fill (core.py:228) is the POST-fill position, not the pre-fill position the overfill check `is_overfill = would_go_negative(current_quantity, side, quantity)` (core.py:351, position.py:144 `quantity > current_quantity`) was designed against. Concretely: position=100, SELL 100 to flatten. record_envelope_fill folds to position=0. append_fill then reads current_quantity=0, calls would_go_negative(0, SELL, 100) = 100>0 = True → is_overfill=True → a fill_overfill_quarantined audit event (payload quarantined=True) is fabricated for a clean full exit. This reproduces for any leg quantity > (pre-fill remaining)/2, not just full flattens. The actual position stays correct (0, not negative) because the fold only counts the FILL event once (dedupe on the shared dedupe_key prevents append_fill's shadow event from double-appending — confirmed via _append_execution_event_unlocked's dedupe_key check at memory.py:2541-2545), and quarantined_symbols (projectors.py:141, checking whether the fold ever goes negative) is correctly not tripped since the fold never actually crosses through negative — only the injected is_overfill flag in plan_append_fill is wrong because it's fed contaminated current_quantity. Verified the identical current_quantity-after-write pattern exists in both memory.py (line 2393) and sqlite.py (line 3599), so both stores mislabel identically (H10 parity preserved, explaining why pytest is green — no test likely asserts fill_overfill_quarantined absence on a clean full-quantity envelope exit). This is a real, reproducible audit-integrity defect (H5/INV-085 concern: the "overfill" fact is fabricated, not faithfully recorded) though it does not corrupt position/quantity state or the real quarantine latch — appropriately P1, not P0, matching the finder's severity.

title: INV-085 overclaims: a broker-authoritative overfill landing after COMPLETED/EXPIRED/EXHAUSTED/SUPERSEDED/CANCELLED never chains to BREACHED — the mandate stays filed as a success
location: app/store/core.py:2542-2563 (plan_envelope_fill's transition-chaining branch); app/transitions.py:59-93 (ENVELOPE_TRANSITIONS: COMPLETED/EXPIRED/EXHAUSTED/SUPERSEDED/CANCELLED all map to set() — no outgoing edge to BREACHED); docs/INVARIANTS.md:643-656 (INV-085)
mechanism: WO-0029A (accepted) added exactly one new edge, FROZEN -> BREACHED, and plan_envelope_fill's chained-transition logic only fires `if envelope.status in (EnvelopeStatus.ACTIVE, EnvelopeStatus.FROZEN)`. Every OTHER terminal status (COMPLETED, EXPIRED, EXHAUSTED, SUPERSEDED, CANCELLED) falls into the function's 'terminal' branch, which only sets `payload["late_fill"]=True` and (if quantity>remaining) `payload["overfill"]=True` on the FILL ExecutionEvent — it never attempts a status transition, and even if it did, ENVELOPE_TRANSITIONS maps every one of those statuses to `set()` (no legal edge to BREACHED exists for them at all). docs/INVARIANTS.md INV-085 states the broader claim verbatim: 'A broker-authoritative overfill of qty_ceiling chains the envelope to BREACHED in every state that can receive a fill' and titles itself 'A ceiling-violated mandate never terminates in the success state.' The code (and WO-0029A's own done_when, which scopes the fix to 'FROZEN + ceiling-overfill chains BREACHED') only delivers this for FROZEN; the invariant document's blanket wording is not honored by the implementation for the other four terminal statuses, one of which (COMPLETED) is literally the 'success state' the invariant's title names.
adversarial_probe: Activate an envelope with qty_ceiling=100. Drive it to remaining=0 via a normal exact fill so it legitimately transitions ACTIVE->COMPLETED (clean, correct completion). Now call `record_envelope_fill(envelope_id, quantity=50, dedupe_key="fill:o1:late-exec", order_id=<same or a stale replaced order>)` — a distinct dedupe_key, e.g. a genuinely separate broker execution report the reconciliation bridge or stream discovers afterward (the same class of 'broker sends more fills than expected' fact ADR-001's order-level overfill-quarantine machinery exists to handle). `plan_envelope_fill` records the fill, sets `overfill=True`/`overfill_quantity=50` in the FILL event payload, floors remaining at 0 — but `envelope.status` stays COMPLETED. `store.get_envelope(envelope_id).status is EnvelopeStatus.COMPLETED` remains true forever; nothing in ENVELOPE_TRANSITIONS permits COMPLETED->BREACHED even if a caller tried. An operator or downstream automation that gates on envelope.status (rather than manually re-deriving overfill facts from every FILL event's payload) sees a clean, successful mandate despite a real, broker-confirmed sell in excess of the human-approved ceiling — exactly the failure INV-085/REV-0023 SPEC-05 was written to close, just via the COMPLETED/EXPIRED/EXHAUSTED/SUPERSEDED/CANCELLED door instead of the FROZEN one WO-0029A patched. Confirmed untested: `tests/test_wo0016_envelope_fills.py::test_late_fill_on_terminal_envelope_is_recorded_not_hidden` only exercises CANCELLED with a NON-overfilling quantity (10 against remaining 100) and never asserts status for a genuinely overfilling late fill on any terminal status other than FROZEN.

title: record_envelope_fill's unchecked Optional[price] can durably write a priceless FILL event that permanently breaks position projection for the symbol

The only place price=None is actually exercised is a test helper that never reads position back afterward, so it does not itself crash today. This is therefore a real defense-in-depth / API-contract gap in a public StateStore method (worth fixing -- add a fill_value_reason(quantity, price) check to plan_envelope_fill mirroring plan_append_fill, and tighten the signature to require price) but not a defect with a live trigger anywhere in the current call graph, so P2 (mechanism gap / weak guard) is the accurate severity rather than P1.

Traced the full path at tip and reproduced it directly against the live InMemoryStateStore (not just read the code): activate an envelope, call `record_envelope_fill(env.id, quantity=30, dedupe_key=..., order_id="o1")` with `price` omitted (defaults to None). `plan_envelope_fill` (app/store/core.py:2452) does zero value validation on `price` — unlike `plan_append_fill`, which gates on `fill_value_reason(quantity, price)` before writing anything. The store's `record_envelope_fill` (app/store/memory.py:1119, and the identical shape in app/store/sqlite.py) applies the plan unconditionally: `remaining_quantity` decrements to 70 and a `FILL` `ExecutionEvent` with `price=None` is durably appended to the event log. The very next call to `store.get_position('AAPL')` folds the event log via `project_symbol_position` -> `_fill_from_event` (app/events/projectors.py), which treats `price` as a required field and raises `ProjectionError: FILL event sequence=... missing required field(s): price`. My repro script reproduced exactly this: `remaining after priceless fill: 70` followed by `get_position RAISED: ProjectionError ...`. Because FILL events are append-only and position is derived solely from the log, this poisons every future `get_position`/`list_positions`/`close_session` snapshot for that symbol with no retraction path — a durable, unrecoverable crash from a single bad call. I also confirmed the finder's own caveat: the two production callers in app/monitoring.py (`_apply_update`/`_apply_inferred_fills`) source price from `BrokerFill.price`/`InferredFill.price`, which are both required non-Optional floats (app/broker/adapter.py:85, app/reconciliation.py's InferredFill), so the *shipped* fill-ingest paths cannot trigger this today — it requires either a future/test caller passing `price=None` or a malformed broker/report value slipping through before reaching this method. I further confirmed that `tests/test_wo0016_envelope_fills.py::test_duplicate_fill_is_counted_exactly_once` (lines 90-95) already calls `record_envelope_fill` with `price` omitted and it passes today only because that test never calls `get_position` afterward — i.e. this exact gap is already latent in a merged, passing test file. This is a real, concrete, mechanically-confirmed defect (missing input validation on a public StateStore write method whose only caller-side guard is currently accidental), not merely theoretical. Severity: P1 is appropriate, not P0 — it does not by itself cause a silent wrong position, kill-switch bypass, or oversell; it fails loud (raises) rather than silent, and is not reachable through any currently-wired production ingestion path (both BrokerFill and InferredFill require price). But it's a correctness/robustness gap with a real, demonstrated trigger (an existing test already exercises the missing-price shape) and a severe blast radius if triggered (permanent unrecoverable position-read poisoning for the symbol), consistent with the finder's own P1 rating.
votes.counter_probe: Executed against real code: InMemoryStateStore().record_envelope_fill(env.id, quantity=30, dedupe_key="fill:o1:x", order_id="o1") with price omitted -> succeeded, remaining_quantity=70; immediately following store.get_position("AAPL") raised ProjectionError("FILL event sequence=4 missing required field(s): price"). No guard in plan_envelope_fill (app/store/core.py) or in either store's record_envelope_fill blocks a None/invalid price before the event is durably appended.

title: Sell-side tape screen has no magnitude / out-of-range guard: a single finite-but-absurd historical print permanently drives the working stop and forces spurious protective SUBMITs
location: app/sellside/policy.py:69 _snapshot_invalid_reasons; consumed via decide tape filter (policy.py:304) and trails.py:98 ref_high = max(b.high for b in prefix)
mechanism: H6 / INV-086 / SOL-F-003 and the CLAUDE.md safety rail claim the WHOLE active tape is screened for 'stale/NaN/negative/crossed/out-of-range' so invalid data never drives sizing/stops/regime/submission. _snapshot_invalid_reasons only rejects None / non-finite / <=0 / crossed-quote / stale — it has NO magnitude or deviation-from-prev_close band. A finite, positive, non-crossed, non-stale but absurd print (fat-finger / feed glitch, a real event in the extended-hours penny universe this system targets) therefore passes every screen, is aggregated into a completed (immutable) bar, and — because trails.py recomputes ref_high = max(high) over the WHOLE prefix at every step and the ratchet takes a running max — permanently pins ref_high to the absurd value. ws.stop = ref_high - distance becomes absurdly high for the envelope's entire lifetime, so decide()'s breakdown trigger `latest.last_price <= ws.stop` is perpetually TRUE, emitting a stop_triggered SUBMIT (marketable SELL at the bid) on every eligible tick. Invalid market data thus drives submission, the exact safety-rail the invariant forbids, and self-reinforces (the poisoned bar never leaves the running max).
adversarial_probe: Build a tape of ~20 valid 30s-spaced snapshots around last_price≈$2.00 (bid<ask, non-stale, positive volume) so bars>=MIN_CLASSIFY_BARS, but make ONE mid-tape completed-bucket snapshot last_price=1_000_000.0 (finite, positive, non-crossed, non-stale). _snapshot_invalid_reasons returns () for it, so it survives the tape filter. aggregate() yields a bar with high=1e6; compute_working_stop ratchets ref_high=1e6 → ws.stop≈1e6. decide() with a normal latest snapshot (last_price=2.0) evaluates 2.0 <= ~1e6 → returns PlannedAction(kind=SUBMIT, stop_triggered=True, limit_price=bid). Every subsequent tick repeats it because ref_high stays 1e6 (running max over the immutable poisoned bar).

votes.reasoning: Traced the actual code path. In app/sellside/policy.py:69-94, `_snapshot_invalid_reasons` (applied both to the latest-snapshot gate at line ~285 and, per WO-0031, to every historical row via the tape filter at line ~304 `not _snapshot_invalid_reasons(s)`) only rejects: stale, non-finite/<=0 last_price/bid/ask, invalid volume, and bid>=ask (crossed_quote). There is no check bounding last_price against bid/ask magnitude, prior closes, or any absolute/relative sanity band anywhere in the pipeline (confirmed absent in app/marketdata/service.py, app/monitoring.py, app/sellside/bars.py, indicators.py). A snapshot with last_price=1_000_000.0 but bid/ask left at normal ~2.00 values (bid<ask, so not crossed) passes `_snapshot_invalid_reasons` cleanly and enters the screened tape. app/sellside/bars.py:aggregate only checks `_finite_positive(last_price)` for bucket membership (no cross-check against bid/ask), so it builds a bar with `high=max(prices)`, i.e. high=1e6, from that one snapshot. app/sellside/trails.py:98 `ref_high = max(b.high for b in prefix)` takes the running max over the full bar prefix, so once that poisoned bar exists in the tape it permanently pins ref_high≈1e6 for the rest of the envelope's lifetime (bars are recomputed each tick from the same retained snapshot history, and the ratchet is a running max with no decay/eviction). compute_working_stop derives ws.stop from ref_high minus a distance, so the stop becomes ~1e6, and decide()'s breakdown check `latest.last_price <= ws.stop` (normal price ~$2) is perpetually true, forcing a stop_triggered SUBMIT every eligible tick. This is a genuine gap: invalid-but-finite/positive/non-crossed market data (a fat-finger print) drives sizing/stops/submission, which is exactly what H6/INV-086 and the CLAUDE.md safety rail ('invalid market data ... must halt or quarantine ... never drive sizing or submission') forbid. Severity P2 is reasonable — it's a real, reproducible defect but requires a specific out-of-band print to trigger, it fails safe in the conservative direction for a long-sell strategy (forces protective selling rather than causing an oversell/hidden loss), and it doesn't break dual-store parity, kill-switch, or fill/position integrity.
votes.counter_probe: Constructed trace: 20 normal snapshots (last_price≈2.00, bid=1.99/ask=2.01, non-stale, volume increasing) interleaved with one snapshot at last_price=1_000_000.0, bid=1.99, ask=2.01 (unchanged), non-stale, valid volume. `_snapshot_invalid_reasons` on this snapshot: stale=False; last_price finite&>0 → no last_price_invalid; bid/ask unchanged and finite&>0 → no bid/ask_invalid; volume valid; bid(1.99) < ask(2.01) → crossed_quote=False. Returns () — snapshot is valid and survives the WO-0031 tape screen. It lands in its own aggregation bucket (or shares one with normal ticks) giving bar.high=1e6 (or at least a bucket max inflated to 1e6 if it's alone in its 30s bucket). trails.py's ref_high=max(b.high for b in prefix) over all subsequent calls includes that bar forever (bars/tape are rebuilt fresh each tick from the full retained snapshot history with no expiry), so ws.stop stays pinned near 1e6, and decide()'s `latest.last_price <= ws.stop` (2.00 <= ~1e6) fires SUBMIT on every subsequent eligible tick. No guard in the cited files intercepts this — confirmed by grep showing zero magnitude/deviation checks in marketdata/service.py, monitoring.py, bars.py, policy.py, indicators.py.

title: stage_envelope_action orders the session-ensure call differently in the two stores, so an unknown envelope_id can leave a persisted session/event artifact in SqliteStateStore that InMemoryStateStore never writes
mechanism: InMemoryStateStore.stage_envelope_action looks up the envelope FIRST and raises UnknownEntityError before ever calling _ensure_current_session_unlocked(). SqliteStateStore.stage_envelope_action calls _ensure_current_session_locked() FIRST (outside/before the envelope SELECT, and in its own nested `with self._tx()` block that commits independently of the outer transaction), and only afterwards opens the outer `with self._tx() as cur:` to look up the envelope and raise UnknownEntityError. _ensure_current_session_locked/_unlocked is normally a no-op read once today's session exists (both stores' initialize() calls it once at startup), so this ordering difference is invisible in ordinary operation -- but on the very first store call after a wall-clock date rollover past midnight with no intervening call, if that first call happens to be stage_envelope_action with a bad/unknown envelope_id, SqliteStateStore durably persists a brand-new SessionRecord row plus a 'session_opened' audit event as a side effect of a call that ultimately raises, while InMemoryStateStore raises the identical UnknownEntityError with zero persisted side effects. Both stores are supposed to 'accept/reject/event ... IDENTICALLY for every envelope operation' (H10); here the raised exception matches but the durable audit trail and session table differ for the same rejected input. This exact ordering is unchanged by the current diff's hunks (pre-existing since WO-0019), but stage_envelope_action itself is a method explicitly named in-scope for this lens and was otherwise modified in this same delta (STAGE_REFUSED_STALE branch, reduce-only current_position wiring), so its behavior is squarely part of the reviewed surface.
adversarial_probe: Freshly construct and .initialize() both an InMemoryStateStore and a SqliteStateStore at 23:59:59 UTC on day N (so each has 'day N' as its current session). Advance the wall clock (or mock utcnow()) to 00:00:01 UTC on day N+1 with no other store call in between. Call `await store.stage_envelope_action('nonexistent-id', some_action, snapshot_fingerprint='fp')` on each. Both raise UnknownEntityError identically, but `await sqlite_store.list_sessions()` now shows TWO session rows (day N and a freshly-created day N+1) with a session_opened event for day N+1, while `await memory_store.list_sessions()` still shows only ONE (day N) -- the same rejected input produced different persisted state.

votes.reasoning: Traced both stage_envelope_action implementations at tip. InMemoryStateStore (app/store/memory.py ~1214-1220): under async with self._lock, it does `env = self._envelopes.get(envelope_id)` and raises UnknownEntityError immediately if None -- _ensure_current_session_unlocked() is only called afterward, so on an unknown envelope_id no session bootstrap ever runs. SqliteStateStore (app/store/sqlite.py ~2132-2138): under async with self._lock, it calls `session = self._ensure_current_session_locked()` FIRST, then opens a separate `with self._tx() as cur:` block to SELECT the envelope and raise UnknownEntityError if the row is missing. Critically, _ensure_current_session_locked (sqlite.py:1039-1060) has its own independent `with self._tx() as cur:` that INSERTs the new SessionRecord and a 'session_opened' event and COMMITs on success before returning -- this is a separate SQLite transaction from the outer one used for the envelope lookup. Both stores' initialize() call the session-ensure helper once at startup (sqlite.py:458+, memory.py:184-190), so a session for 'day N' exists in both after construction. If the wall clock (utcnow()) rolls to day N+1 with no other store call in between, and the first call on each store post-rollover is stage_envelope_action with a nonexistent envelope_id: SqliteStateStore's _ensure_current_session_locked sees no day-N+1 row, inserts+commits a new session + session_opened event (durably, in its own already-committed transaction), and only then proceeds to the outer transaction's envelope SELECT, which finds nothing and raises UnknownEntityError (which only rolls back the outer, empty transaction -- the session insert is unaffected since it already committed in a prior, distinct transaction). InMemoryStateStore never reaches _ensure_current_session_unlocked at all because the envelope lookup and raise happen first. Both stores raise identical UnknownEntityError to the caller, but list_sessions() afterward shows two rows (day N, day N+1) with a session_opened audit event for SqliteStateStore and only one row for InMemoryStateStore -- confirming the H10 parity break exactly as described. This is a genuine, narrow-window (midnight-rollover-first-call) defect; severity P2 is appropriate since no funds/position/order state is affected, only session-table/audit-log parity.
votes.counter_probe: sqlite.py:1039-1060 _ensure_current_session_locked's own `with self._tx() as cur:` commits the new session row + session_opened event independently BEFORE stage_envelope_action's outer `with self._tx() as cur:` (sqlite.py:2135+) even begins its SELECT on execution_envelopes; memory.py's stage_envelope_action does `env = self._envelopes.get(envelope_id)` and raises before ever calling `self._ensure_current_session_unlocked()` (memory.py:1214-1220), so no equivalent session-creation side effect occurs there for the same rejected call.

title: New facade envelope Protocol members return Any, not the concrete type
mechanism: Unlike the WO-0030 StateStore ABC lift (whose 8 envelope methods are typed with concrete return types so a store that drops/mistypes a method now fails `mypy app/`), the three envelope members newly added to the facade Protocols are typed to return `Any`. mypy still catches a missing method or a wrong parameter name on the concrete facade (structural conformance is checked wherever StoreBackedQueryFacade/StoreBackedCommandFacade are returned typed as the Protocol, e.g. app/api/deps.py get_query_facade/get_command_facade), but it will NOT catch a return-type regression at this seam the way it now does at the store seam.
adversarial_probe: Change StoreBackedCommandFacade.cancel_envelope's return statement to return a plain dict instead of the ExecutionEnvelope model and run `mypy app/`: it stays green because the Protocol return annotation is Any, whereas an equivalent regression at StateStore.record_envelope_fill would fail mypy immediately since that ABC method is typed concretely. Not a behavior bug today -- the concrete implementations are correct -- but it is an inconsistency in how much the WO-0030 lift actually hardens each seam.

votes.reasoning: Verified directly in the files. app/store/base.py's StateStore ABC types envelope methods concretely (e.g. get_envelope -> Optional[ExecutionEnvelope], list_envelopes -> list[ExecutionEnvelope], record_envelope_fill and friends similarly concrete), so any concrete store subclass that mistypes/regresses a return value is caught by `mypy app/` via LSP-violation on override. In contrast app/facade/commands.py's ExecutionCommandFacade Protocol declares approve_envelope/cancel_envelope (lines ~130-142) as `-> Any`, and app/facade/queries.py's ExecutionQueryFacade declares list_envelopes (lines 27-31) as `-> Any` — confirmed by direct grep/read of both files. app/api/deps.py's get_query_facade/get_command_facade return the concrete StoreBackedQueryFacade/StoreBackedCommandFacade typed as the Protocol (confirmed lines 92-94, 118-122), so mypy does check structural conformance (missing methods, wrong signatures) at that seam, but because the Protocol's declared return type is Any, a return-type regression in the concrete facade implementation (e.g. returning a dict instead of ExecutionEnvelope) would not be flagged by mypy the way an equivalent regression in a StateStore override would be. This is exactly the asymmetry the finding describes. It is a real but non-behavioral (today's implementations are correct) type-hardening gap — correctly scored as P3/nit, not a safety-core violation.
votes.counter_probe: Read app/store/base.py envelope method signatures (concrete return types) vs app/facade/commands.py:129-142 and app/facade/queries.py:27-31 (Any-typed) and app/api/deps.py:92-122 (Protocol-typed return sites where structural checking does occur) — all three match the finding's claims exactly with no guard or exception found that would narrow the facade Protocol's Any return types.
